name: Pigeonhole Principle
one_liner: If more objects than boxes, some box holds at least two.
trigger: forcing a coincidence or repetition
example_crux: Among any n+1 numbers from {1,...,2n} two are coprime because consecutive integers share a box.

name: Extremal Principle
one_liner: Consider a maximal or minimal configuration to derive contradictions or structure.
trigger: take the largest/smallest such object
example_crux: Assume the minimal counterexample and show a smaller one exists, forcing none to exist.

name: Double Counting
one_liner: Count one quantity two ways to equate two expressions.
trigger: count incidences from two sides
example_crux: Summing degrees counts edges twice, giving the handshake identity.

name: Bijective Proof
one_liner: Establish a one-to-one correspondence between two sets to equate their sizes.
trigger: prove two counts are equal constructively
example_crux: Binary strings map to subsets, showing there are 2^n subsets of an n-set.

name: Generating Functions
one_liner: Encode a sequence as coefficients of a formal power series to manipulate it.
trigger: a recurrence or convolution of counts
example_crux: Partitions into distinct parts equal partitions into odd parts via a product identity of series.

name: Roots of Unity Filter
one_liner: Extract coefficients in an arithmetic progression by averaging over roots of unity.
trigger: count objects with size divisible by k
example_crux: Number of subsets with sum divisible by 3 comes from evaluating (1+x)^n at cube roots of unity.

name: Burnside's Lemma
one_liner: Counts orbits as the average number of points fixed by group elements.
trigger: count configurations up to symmetry
example_crux: Colorings of a necklace up to rotation equal the average fixed colorings over all rotations.

name: Pólya Enumeration
one_liner: Uses the cycle index of a symmetry group to count colorings by weight.
trigger: count colorings up to symmetry with color multiplicities
example_crux: Substituting color sums into the cycle index enumerates inequivalent bead arrangements.

name: Inclusion-Exclusion
one_liner: Corrects overcounting by alternating sums over intersections of properties.
trigger: count objects avoiding several forbidden properties
example_crux: Derangements arise from subtracting permutations fixing each subset of points.

name: Probabilistic Method
one_liner: Show an object exists by proving a random construction has positive probability of success.
trigger: prove existence without explicit construction
example_crux: A random 2-coloring shows Ramsey-type lower bounds since bad events have total probability below one.

name: Linearity of Expectation
one_liner: The expected sum equals the sum of expectations regardless of dependence.
trigger: compute an average count of something
example_crux: Expected number of fixed points of a random permutation is one by summing per-point probabilities.

name: Second Moment Method
one_liner: Bounds the probability a random variable is near zero using its variance.
trigger: show a random count is almost surely positive
example_crux: Variance control shows random graphs contain a subgraph with high probability near the threshold.

name: Lovász Local Lemma
one_liner: Avoids all bad events when each is rare and sparsely dependent.
trigger: many rare, locally-dependent bad events
example_crux: A proper coloring exists because each edge's bad event depends on few others.

name: Deletion / Alteration Method
one_liner: Build a random structure then remove a few bad parts to fix it.
trigger: random object almost works, delete defects
example_crux: Deleting one vertex per short cycle yields graphs with high girth and large independence.

name: Invariant
one_liner: Find a quantity preserved by all allowed moves to constrain reachability.
trigger: prove a final state is unreachable
example_crux: A parity or coloring preserved by moves shows the target position cannot be attained.

name: Monovariant
one_liner: Track a quantity that strictly changes in one direction to prove termination or bounds.
trigger: process must terminate or stabilize
example_crux: A strictly decreasing nonnegative potential forces the chip-firing process to halt.

name: Coloring Argument
one_liner: Assign colors to expose parity or divisibility obstructions to tilings or moves.
trigger: tiling or covering feasibility
example_crux: A checkerboard coloring shows a mutilated board cannot be tiled by dominoes.

name: Telescoping
one_liner: Write terms as consecutive differences so a sum collapses.
trigger: a sum with a difference-of-terms form
example_crux: Partial fractions turn a rational sum into a telescoping cancellation.

name: Recurrence / Recursion
one_liner: Relate a count to smaller instances and solve or bound the relation.
trigger: count builds from smaller cases
example_crux: Tilings of a 2×n strip satisfy the Fibonacci recurrence by conditioning on the last piece.

name: Stars and Bars
one_liner: Count nonnegative integer solutions as arrangements of stars and dividers.
trigger: distribute identical items into bins
example_crux: Solutions to x1+...+xk=n number C(n+k-1,k-1) by placing k-1 bars among n stars.

name: Reflection Principle
one_liner: Count bad paths by reflecting across a boundary to a countable set.
trigger: paths crossing a barrier (ballot problems)
example_crux: Ballot sequences are counted by subtracting reflected bad paths from all paths.

name: Cycle Lemma
one_liner: Exactly one cyclic rotation of a sequence stays positive, counting good arrangements.
trigger: cyclic sequences with a positivity condition
example_crux: Dvoretzky–Motzkin's rotation gives Catalan numbers as one good rotation per cycle class.

name: Chung–Feller Theorem
one_liner: Paths of fixed length have the same count regardless of steps below the axis.
trigger: uniform distribution of a path statistic
example_crux: The number of Dyck-type paths with 2k steps below the axis is independent of k.

name: Catalan Number Toolkit
one_liner: Recognize and count structures enumerated by Catalan numbers.
trigger: balanced/nested/noncrossing structures
example_crux: Triangulations, Dyck paths, and balanced parentheses share the Catalan count via bijections.

name: Chicken McNugget (Frobenius)
one_liner: Largest amount not representable by coprime coins a,b is ab-a-b.
trigger: nonnegative combinations of coprime numbers
example_crux: With coins 3 and 5 the largest unattainable value is 7 by the Frobenius formula.

name: Sign-Reversing Involution
one_liner: Pair terms of opposite sign to cancel and evaluate an alternating sum.
trigger: prove an alternating identity combinatorially
example_crux: An involution cancels all but a few terms to prove a determinant or PIE identity.

name: Lindström–Gessel–Viennot Lemma
one_liner: Counts nonintersecting path families as a determinant of path counts.
trigger: nonintersecting lattice paths or tilings
example_crux: Plane partitions count via a determinant of binomial path counts from sources to sinks.

name: Prüfer Sequences
one_liner: Encode labeled trees as sequences to count them.
trigger: counting labeled trees
example_crux: Cayley's formula n^{n-2} follows from a bijection with length n-2 label sequences.

name: Cayley's Formula
one_liner: There are n^{n-2} labeled trees on n vertices.
trigger: enumerate spanning or labeled trees
example_crux: Counting rooted forests or using Prüfer codes yields the labeled tree count.

name: Matrix-Tree Theorem
one_liner: Spanning trees equal any cofactor of the graph Laplacian.
trigger: count spanning trees of a graph
example_crux: A Laplacian cofactor gives the number of spanning trees of the complete graph.

name: Hall's Marriage Theorem
one_liner: A perfect matching exists iff every set of vertices has enough neighbors.
trigger: system of distinct representatives / matchings
example_crux: A Latin rectangle extends because each column's neighborhood satisfies Hall's condition.

name: Dilworth's Theorem
one_liner: Minimum chain cover of a poset equals its largest antichain.
trigger: partition into monotone parts / antichains
example_crux: Erdős–Szekeres follows from covering a sequence by monotone subsequences.

name: Erdős–Szekeres
one_liner: Any sequence of more than (r-1)(s-1) terms has a long monotone subsequence.
trigger: guaranteed monotone subsequence
example_crux: Labeling each term by its longest increasing run forces a repeat by pigeonhole.

name: Sperner's Theorem
one_liner: Largest antichain of subsets is the middle binomial coefficient.
trigger: family with no subset relations
example_crux: Counting maximal chains through each set bounds an antichain by C(n,n/2).

name: Sperner's Lemma
one_liner: A properly labeled triangulation contains a full-color small triangle.
trigger: discrete fixed-point / fair division
example_crux: Counting rainbow edges parity-forces an odd number of tricolored cells.

name: Turán's Theorem
one_liner: Maximizes edges in a graph with no complete subgraph of given size.
trigger: edge bound forbidding a clique
example_crux: A triangle-free graph on n vertices has at most n^2/4 edges (Mantel).

name: Kővári–Sós–Turán
one_liner: Bounds edges in graphs avoiding a complete bipartite subgraph.
trigger: forbid K_{s,t} / Zarankovic problem
example_crux: Counting cherries bounds edges when no two vertices share t common neighbors.

name: Ramsey Theory
one_liner: Large enough structures always contain a monochromatic ordered substructure.
trigger: unavoidable monochromatic pattern
example_crux: Any 2-coloring of K_6's edges contains a monochromatic triangle.

name: Erdős–Ko–Rado
one_liner: Largest intersecting family of k-subsets fixes one element.
trigger: pairwise intersecting set family bound
example_crux: A cyclic permutation counting argument bounds an intersecting family by C(n-1,k-1).

name: Cauchy–Davenport
one_liner: Sumset in Z_p has size at least min(p, |A|+|B|-1).
trigger: lower bound a modular sumset
example_crux: Adding two residue sets mod p forces many distinct sums, bounding restricted sums.

name: Combinatorial Nullstellensatz
one_liner: A polynomial with a nonzero top-degree coefficient has a nonvanishing point on a grid.
trigger: existence via a nonzero coefficient
example_crux: Regular graphs have proper list-colorings by finding a nonvanishing grid point of a polynomial.

name: Transfer Matrix Method
one_liner: Count paths/configurations along a line via powers of a transition matrix.
trigger: linear sequence of local states
example_crux: Tilings of a strip are counted by entries of a transfer matrix raised to n.

name: Vandermonde's Identity
one_liner: Sum of products of binomials equals a single binomial via committee choice.
trigger: convolution of binomial coefficients
example_crux: Choosing a k-committee from two groups sums split choices to C(m+n,k).

name: Hockey Stick Identity
one_liner: A diagonal sum of binomials collapses to one binomial.
trigger: sum of binomials with fixed lower index
example_crux: Summing C(k,r) for k up to n yields C(n+1,r+1) by Pascal telescoping.

name: Snake Oil Method
one_liner: Prove a combinatorial identity by summing its generating function and simplifying.
trigger: identity with a free parameter to sum over
example_crux: Multiplying an unknown sum by x^n and summing collapses to a known series.

name: WZ Method
one_liner: Certifies hypergeometric identities via a companion telescoping term.
trigger: prove a closed-form binomial-sum identity
example_crux: A rational certificate proves a binomial identity by verifying a telescoping recurrence.

name: Deletion–Contraction
one_liner: Express a graph invariant recursively by removing or contracting an edge.
trigger: compute chromatic/Tutte type invariants
example_crux: The chromatic polynomial satisfies P(G)=P(G-e)-P(G/e).

name: Euler's Formula for Planar Graphs
one_liner: For connected planar graphs, V-E+F=2.
trigger: planarity or face-counting bounds
example_crux: Combining Euler's relation with edge-face inequalities proves K5 is nonplanar.

name: Discharging Method
one_liner: Assign and redistribute charges to force a local unavoidable structure.
trigger: sparse planar structural results
example_crux: Charges summing to a negative total force an unavoidable low-degree configuration.

name: Compression / Shifting
one_liner: Transform a set family toward a canonical form without worsening the objective.
trigger: reduce an extremal family to a simple shape
example_crux: Shifting an intersecting family to a compressed one preserves size for EKR.

name: Smoothing / Adjustment
one_liner: Move toward the extremum by local adjustments that improve the objective.
trigger: optimize a symmetric configuration
example_crux: Equalizing unequal variables increases a product, forcing the optimum at equality.

name: Averaging Argument
one_liner: Some instance meets the average, guaranteeing a good or bad case.
trigger: prove an object exceeding the mean exists
example_crux: Some row of a matrix has sum at least the overall average.

name: Generating Trees / ECO
one_liner: Build objects by adding one active site at a time and track succession rules.
trigger: objects grow with labeled active sites
example_crux: Pattern-avoiding permutations are enumerated via a succession rule on insertion slots.

name: Zeckendorf Representation
one_liner: Every integer is a unique sum of nonconsecutive Fibonacci numbers.
trigger: Fibonacci-base encoding of integers
example_crux: Greedy selection of Fibonacci numbers yields the unique nonconsecutive representation.

name: Handshake / Degree-Sum Lemma
one_liner: The sum of vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges.
trigger: relate degrees to edges or parity
example_crux: The number of odd-degree vertices must be even.

name: Local Moves / Reconfiguration
one_liner: Show configurations are connected or fixed under small allowed moves.
trigger: reach one configuration from another by steps
example_crux: Adjacent transpositions connect all permutations, so any target is reachable.

name: Linearity of Expectation
one_liner: The expected value of a sum equals the sum of expected values, regardless of dependence.
trigger: computing an expected count of something
example_crux: Expected number of fixed points of a random permutation is n·(1/n) = 1 by summing indicator expectations.

name: Indicator Random Variables
one_liner: Represent a count as a sum of 0/1 variables whose expectation is a probability.
trigger: counting occurrences of events
example_crux: Expected number of records in a random sequence is the sum over k of 1/k.

name: Probabilistic Method
one_liner: Prove an object exists by showing a random construction has positive probability of the desired property.
trigger: an existence claim about combinatorial structures
example_crux: A random 2-coloring shows a Ramsey-type bound because the expected number of monochromatic sets is below 1.

name: First Moment Method
one_liner: If the expected count of bad events is less than one, some outcome has none of them.
trigger: showing a configuration avoiding all bad events exists
example_crux: Expected number of long monochromatic runs under 1 forces a coloring with no such run.

name: Second Moment Method
one_liner: Use variance to show a nonnegative variable is positive with high probability.
trigger: proving something occurs almost surely, not just in expectation
example_crux: Bounding variance shows the number of triangles in a random graph is concentrated near its mean.

name: Union Bound
one_liner: The probability of a union is at most the sum of the individual probabilities.
trigger: bounding the chance any bad event happens
example_crux: Summing failure probabilities over all cliques shows a good coloring exists.

name: Inclusion-Exclusion (Probability)
one_liner: Compute the probability of a union via alternating sums of intersection probabilities.
trigger: at least one of several overlapping events
example_crux: Derangement probability arises from alternating sums of fixing k specified points.

name: Complementary Counting
one_liner: Compute a probability by subtracting the probability of its complement from one.
trigger: the complement is easier to count
example_crux: Probability of at least one shared birthday is one minus the all-distinct probability.

name: Bayes' Theorem
one_liner: Reverse a conditional probability using priors and likelihoods.
trigger: updating a probability given evidence
example_crux: Posterior that a chosen coin is biased follows from its higher likelihood of the observed flips.

name: Law of Total Probability
one_liner: Average conditional probabilities over a partition of the sample space.
trigger: splitting on the first outcome or a hidden case
example_crux: Condition on the first draw to set up a recursion for the target event.

name: Tower Property (Iterated Expectation)
one_liner: The expectation of a conditional expectation equals the unconditional expectation.
trigger: a two-stage random experiment
example_crux: Condition on the random number of trials to compute total expected successes.

name: First-Step Analysis
one_liner: Set up equations for probabilities or expectations by conditioning on the first transition.
trigger: random walks or processes on states
example_crux: Expected hitting time satisfies a linear system from conditioning on the first step.

name: State Diagram / Markov Chain Setup
one_liner: Model the process as transitions among a finite set of states and solve.
trigger: a process whose future depends only on current state
example_crux: Grouping equivalent configurations into a few states collapses the recursion.

name: Gambler's Ruin
one_liner: Compute ruin/hit probabilities and durations for a walk between two barriers.
trigger: a symmetric or biased walk with absorbing endpoints
example_crux: Absorption probability is linear in starting position for the symmetric walk.

name: Optional Stopping Theorem
one_liner: A martingale's expectation at a stopping time equals its initial value under mild conditions.
trigger: a fair process with a natural stopping rule
example_crux: Applying stopping to the walk martingale yields exit probabilities instantly.

name: Wald's Identity
one_liner: The expected sum of a random number of i.i.d. terms is the product of the two expectations.
trigger: summing over a random stopping count
example_crux: Expected total winnings equal expected number of rounds times expected per-round gain.

name: Markov's Inequality
one_liner: Bound the probability a nonnegative variable exceeds a threshold by its mean over the threshold.
trigger: a tail bound from just the expectation
example_crux: A variable with small mean rarely exceeds a large value, forcing a good event.

name: Chebyshev's Inequality
one_liner: Bound deviation from the mean using the variance.
trigger: showing concentration around the mean
example_crux: Small variance forces the count to stay near its mean with high probability.

name: Chernoff Bound
one_liner: Exponentially bound tail probabilities of sums of independent bounded variables.
trigger: very sharp concentration of a sum
example_crux: Exponential moment bounds show almost all coin sequences are nearly balanced.

name: Jensen's Inequality (Probabilistic)
one_liner: A convex function of an expectation is at most the expectation of the function.
trigger: comparing E[f(X)] with f(E[X])
example_crux: Convexity gives a lower bound on an expected product via its expected inputs.

name: Tail Sum Formula for Expectation
one_liner: Expectation of a nonnegative integer variable equals the sum of P(X ≥ k).
trigger: computing E[X] when tail probabilities are simpler
example_crux: Expected maximum of draws follows from summing survival probabilities.

name: Symmetry Argument
one_liner: Exploit interchangeability of outcomes to equate probabilities without computation.
trigger: outcomes that are equally likely by relabeling
example_crux: By symmetry each of the n cards is equally likely to be last, so the answer is 1/n.

name: Geometric Probability
one_liner: Compute probabilities as ratios of lengths, areas, or volumes.
trigger: uniform random points in a region
example_crux: Probability two random meeting times overlap is an area ratio in the unit square.

name: Reflection Principle
one_liner: Count walks touching a barrier by reflecting paths across it.
trigger: walks constrained to stay above/below a line
example_crux: Bad paths crossing a level biject with reflected paths, yielding a clean count.

name: Ballot Problem
one_liner: Probability one candidate leads throughout the count is (a−b)/(a+b).
trigger: one running total staying ahead
example_crux: Persistent-lead paths are counted via the cycle lemma or reflection.

name: Probability Generating Functions
one_liner: Encode a distribution as a power series to extract moments and convolutions.
trigger: sums of independent variables or moment extraction
example_crux: The PGF of a sum is the product of PGFs, giving the distribution of a dice total.

name: Principle of Deferred Decisions
one_liner: Reveal random choices only when needed to simplify analysis.
trigger: a process where later randomness can be postponed
example_crux: Deal cards one at a time as needed rather than fixing the whole shuffle up front.

name: Coupon Collector
one_liner: Expected time to collect all n types is n·H_n via summed geometric waits.
trigger: collecting a full set by random draws
example_crux: Each new coupon phase is geometric with success probability (n−k)/n.

name: Geometric Waiting Time
one_liner: Expected number of independent trials until first success is 1/p.
trigger: repeat until a success occurs
example_crux: Expected rolls to see a six is 1/(1/6) = 6.

name: Pattern Waiting Times (Conway/Penney)
one_liner: Compute expected time until a target string appears using correlation/leading numbers.
trigger: waiting for a specific sequence of outcomes
example_crux: Self-overlap of the target pattern raises the expected waiting time.

name: Lovász Local Lemma
one_liner: If bad events are rare and sparsely dependent, all can be simultaneously avoided.
trigger: many mostly-independent bad events, union bound too weak
example_crux: Local sparsity of clause overlaps guarantees a satisfying assignment exists.

name: Deletion (Alteration) Method
one_liner: Build a random object, then delete the few bad parts to get a good one.
trigger: expected bad count is positive but small
example_crux: Remove one vertex per short cycle to obtain a large high-girth subgraph.

name: Conditioning to Reduce
one_liner: Fix part of the randomness to turn a hard problem into a simpler conditioned one.
trigger: the problem simplifies once a value is known
example_crux: Condition on the total to make the remaining choices uniform.

name: Covariance of Indicators
one_liner: Compute variance of a count by summing pairwise indicator covariances.
trigger: second-moment computations of a sum of indicators
example_crux: Var of fixed-point count comes from covariances between pairs of positions.

name: Order Statistics
one_liner: Analyze the distribution of the k-th smallest among random samples.
trigger: asking about the min, max, or median of draws
example_crux: Expected spacings of n uniform points partition the interval equally by symmetry.

name: Uniform Spacings / Broken Stick
one_liner: n−1 uniform cuts split an interval into n exchangeable pieces.
trigger: breaking an interval at random points
example_crux: Each of the n segments has equal expected length 1/n by exchangeability.

name: Buffon's Needle
one_liner: Relate crossing probability of a dropped needle to geometric integrals over angle and position.
trigger: random line-region intersection probabilities
example_crux: Integrating over uniform angle yields the crossing probability 2L/(πd).

name: Random Permutation Cycle Structure
one_liner: Analyze cycle counts/lengths of a uniform random permutation.
trigger: questions about cycles or fixed points
example_crux: Expected number of cycles is H_n since element k closes a cycle with probability 1/k.

name: Derangement / Fixed-Point Analysis
one_liner: Count permutations with no fixed points via inclusion-exclusion.
trigger: no element maps to itself
example_crux: Probability of a derangement tends to 1/e from the alternating series.

name: Uniform Compositions (Stars and Bars)
one_liner: Model equally likely nonnegative integer solutions to set up uniform probabilities.
trigger: random splits into ordered nonnegative parts
example_crux: Probability a random composition has a fixed first part is a ratio of binomials.

name: Poissonization
one_liner: Replace a fixed count by a Poisson count to decouple dependent bins.
trigger: balls-in-bins with awkward dependence
example_crux: Independent Poisson bin loads simplify occupancy calculations, then de-Poissonize.

name: Poisson Approximation for Rare Events
one_liner: Many rare nearly-independent events give an approximately Poisson count.
trigger: counting rare coincidences among many trials
example_crux: Number of matching birthdays is approximately Poisson with the expected match count.

name: Entropy Method
one_liner: Bound counts or probabilities using Shannon entropy inequalities.
trigger: counting via information content
example_crux: Subadditivity of entropy bounds the number of configurations of a structured set.

name: Coupling
one_liner: Compare two distributions by realizing them on a shared probability space.
trigger: showing one process dominates or matches another
example_crux: Coupling two chains that agree after meeting bounds their distributional distance.

name: Expectation Recursion
one_liner: Set up and solve a recurrence for an expected value by conditioning on one step.
trigger: expected value of an iterated process
example_crux: E_n = 1 + average of E over reachable states solves for expected duration.

name: Self-Referential Expectation (Memoryless Restart)
one_liner: Use that a failed attempt restarts the process to write E in terms of itself.
trigger: process restarts after a specific failure
example_crux: Expected flips for a pattern satisfies E = setup + (prob restart)·E.

name: Linearity Plus Symmetry
one_liner: Combine indicator linearity with symmetry so each term has the same probability.
trigger: a sum of identically-distributed indicators
example_crux: Expected inversions in a random permutation is C(n,2) times the pair probability 1/2.

name: Equiprobable Bijection
one_liner: Match favorable outcomes to a countable set of equally likely outcomes.
trigger: uniform sample space with a natural pairing
example_crux: A rotation bijection shows each cyclic arrangement is equally likely.

name: Variance via E[X²]−E[X]²
one_liner: Compute variance directly from the second and first moments.
trigger: needing spread of a random variable
example_crux: Binomial variance np(1−p) follows from summing independent indicator variances.

name: Negative Hypergeometric / Draw-Until
one_liner: Analyze positions of special items when drawing without replacement.
trigger: draw until the k-th success from a finite pool
example_crux: Expected draws to first ace equals (n+1)/(k+1) by symmetric gap placement.

name: Martingale Construction
one_liner: Build a fair (constant-expectation) process to control a random evolution.
trigger: a quantity whose conditional expectation is unchanged each step
example_crux: Product form makes a biased walk a martingale, giving exit probabilities.

name: Double Counting Expectation
one_liner: Count pairs or incidences two ways to extract an expected quantity.
trigger: average tied to a total incidence count
example_crux: Summing degrees two ways gives the expected number of chosen neighbors.

name: Random Uniform Selection Trick
one_liner: Design or analyze a scheme so each item is chosen with equal probability.
trigger: proving a selection rule is uniform
example_crux: Keeping item k with probability 1/k yields a uniform final choice.

name: State Merging by Symmetry
one_liner: Collapse symmetric states to shrink the transition system before solving.
trigger: a large chain with many equivalent states
example_crux: Grouping positions by distance to the goal reduces the walk to few states.

name: Directed Angle Chasing
one_liner: Tracks angles modulo 180° so configuration cases collapse into one clean computation.
trigger: prove concyclicity or collinearity independent of point order
example_crux: Show four points concyclic by proving two directed angles subtending a chord are equal mod 180°.

name: Power of a Point
one_liner: Relates products of signed segment lengths from a point to a circle.
trigger: a point lies on two chords/secants/tangents of a circle
example_crux: Equate PA·PB = PC·PD to force a fourth point onto a circle.

name: Radical Axis
one_liner: The locus of equal power to two circles is a line perpendicular to their centerline.
trigger: three circles or an unexplained concurrence/perpendicularity of chords
example_crux: Show three chord-lines concur because they are pairwise radical axes.

name: Radical Center
one_liner: The three radical axes of three circles concur at one point.
trigger: prove three lines defined by circles are concurrent
example_crux: Conclude concurrency of three radical axes at the common radical center.

name: Ptolemy's Theorem
one_liner: In a cyclic quadrilateral the product of diagonals equals the sum of products of opposite sides.
trigger: cyclic quadrilateral with a relation among four sides and diagonals
example_crux: Derive a length equality by applying AC·BD = AB·CD + AD·BC on a cyclic quad.

name: Ptolemy's Inequality
one_liner: For any four points AC·BD ≤ AB·CD + AD·BC with equality iff concyclic.
trigger: a four-point length inequality that tightens under concyclicity
example_crux: Bound a distance product and note equality forces the four points onto a circle.

name: Ceva's Theorem
one_liner: Three cevians concur iff a product of three side ratios equals one.
trigger: prove three cevians of a triangle are concurrent
example_crux: Verify the Ceva ratio product equals 1 to get concurrency.

name: Menelaus's Theorem
one_liner: Three points on the sidelines are collinear iff a signed side-ratio product equals −1.
trigger: prove three points on triangle sides are collinear
example_crux: Show a transversal ratio product equals −1 to force collinearity.

name: Trigonometric Ceva
one_liner: Concurrency criterion using ratios of sines of the angles cevians make.
trigger: cevians defined by angles rather than by lengths
example_crux: Prove isogonal cevians concur via the product of sine ratios being 1.

name: Homothety
one_liner: A center-and-ratio scaling that maps figures to similar parallel copies.
trigger: two similar configurations, parallel lines, or tangent circles
example_crux: Map the incircle to an excircle by homothety to relate two tangent points.

name: Spiral Similarity
one_liner: A rotation-plus-scaling about a center taking one segment to another.
trigger: two segments/triangles related by rotation and scaling, shared Miquel point
example_crux: Identify the spiral center sending AB to CD as the second intersection of two circles.

name: Inversion
one_liner: A radius-r map turning circles/lines through the center into lines/circles.
trigger: many circles through one point or tangency conditions
example_crux: Invert at a vertex to turn tangent circles into parallel lines and read off the result.

name: Pole–Polar Duality
one_liner: Pairs points with lines w.r.t. a conic so incidence dualizes.
trigger: tangent lines, harmonic conjugates, or 'prove concurrent/collinear' dual pairs
example_crux: Turn a collinearity of poles into a concurrency of polars through a fixed pole.

name: Cross-Ratio / Projective Invariance
one_liner: Projection preserves the cross-ratio of four collinear or concyclic points.
trigger: perspectivities, pencils of lines, or four special points on a line/conic
example_crux: Equate cross-ratios across a projection to pin down a fourth point.

name: Harmonic Division
one_liner: Uses the special cross-ratio −1 arising from cevians, poles, and midpoints.
trigger: tangents from a point, complete quadrilaterals, or midpoint/infinity relations
example_crux: Show (A,B;C,D) = −1 then project to obtain a midpoint or perpendicular.

name: Complex Number Bash
one_liner: Places points on the unit circle so rotations and concyclicity become algebra.
trigger: circle-heavy config with rotations, reflections, or symmetry
example_crux: Set the circumcircle as |z|=1 and compute the foot/reflection by a closed formula.

name: Barycentric Coordinates
one_liner: Encodes points by mass-ratios relative to a reference triangle.
trigger: triangle centers, cevian ratios, or conic through the vertices
example_crux: Write incenter as (a:b:c) and test collinearity via a determinant.

name: Cartesian Coordinate Bash
one_liner: Assigns coordinates to reduce a synthetic claim to algebra.
trigger: perpendicularity, midpoints, or a stubborn config with few free points
example_crux: Place the right angle at the origin and compute slopes to prove perpendicularity.

name: Trigonometric Bash
one_liner: Expresses lengths/angles via Law of Sines/Cosines and grinds identities.
trigger: angle conditions and length relations mix in one triangle
example_crux: Reduce a length equality to a sine identity via the extended Law of Sines.

name: Vector Method
one_liner: Represents points as vectors so midpoints, centroids, and dot-products compute directly.
trigger: centroid/midpoint relations or perpendicularity via dot products
example_crux: Prove a concurrence by expressing all three points as the same vector combination.

name: Area Ratio Method
one_liner: Turns segment ratios into ratios of triangle areas sharing a base or height.
trigger: ratios along cevians or a 'compute ratio' problem
example_crux: Express [APB]/[APC] as a base ratio to chain segment ratios together.

name: Mass Points
one_liner: Places weights at vertices so balance points give cevian intersection ratios.
trigger: concurrent cevians with unknown division ratios
example_crux: Balance masses at the vertices to read off the ratio in which cevians cut each other.

name: Pick's Theorem
one_liner: Area of a lattice polygon equals interior points plus half boundary points minus one.
trigger: polygon with vertices on integer lattice and an area/count question
example_crux: Compute a lattice polygon's area from its interior and boundary point counts.

name: Shoelace Formula
one_liner: Computes polygon area from vertex coordinates via a cross-product sum.
trigger: area of a polygon with known coordinates
example_crux: Sum coordinate cross terms to get the polygon's signed area.

name: Extremal Principle (Geometry)
one_liner: Considers the largest/smallest distance, angle, or area to force structure.
trigger: combinatorial geometry with 'no three collinear' or extremal configuration
example_crux: Take the closest pair of points and derive a contradiction from an even-closer pair.

name: Symmedian Properties
one_liner: The reflection of the median over the angle bisector has tangent and harmonic characterizations.
trigger: tangent-line intersections at vertices or A-symmedian appears
example_crux: Recognize the line through the tangent intersection as the symmedian to get a ratio.

name: Isogonal Conjugation
one_liner: Reflecting cevians over angle bisectors pairs triangle points into conjugates.
trigger: two points with symmetric angle conditions (e.g. O and H)
example_crux: Show two points are isogonal conjugates to transfer a known property between them.

name: Isotomic Conjugation
one_liner: Reflecting cevian feet over side midpoints pairs points into isotomic conjugates.
trigger: symmetric cevian-foot conditions about midpoints
example_crux: Use isotomic conjugacy to convert one concurrency into an equivalent one.

name: Miquel Point
one_liner: Circles on the sides of a triangle/quadrilateral meet at a common Miquel point.
trigger: four lines or points-on-sides producing several circles
example_crux: Locate the Miquel point of a complete quadrilateral to prove concyclicity.

name: Simson Line
one_liner: Feet of perpendiculars from a circumcircle point to the sides are collinear.
trigger: perpendicular feet from a point on the circumcircle
example_crux: Prove three perpendicular feet collinear by placing the point on the circumcircle.

name: Nine-Point Circle
one_liner: Midpoints, altitude feet, and Euler-point midpoints lie on one circle of half circumradius.
trigger: midpoints and altitude feet appear together
example_crux: Show nine special points share the nine-point circle via a homothety at the orthocenter.

name: Euler Line
one_liner: Circumcenter, centroid, and orthocenter are collinear with OG:GH = 1:2.
trigger: relations among O, G, H of a triangle
example_crux: Use OH = 3·OG collinearity to locate the orthocenter from O and G.

name: Incenter–Excenter Lemma (Fact 5)
one_liner: The arc midpoint is equidistant from two vertices, the incenter, and an excenter.
trigger: incenter, arc midpoint, or excenter on the circumcircle
example_crux: Use the midpoint of arc BC as the center of a circle through B, C, I, and I_A.

name: Phantom Point Method
one_liner: Define a point by a clean property, then prove it coincides with the target.
trigger: the target point has two hard-to-reconcile definitions
example_crux: Construct X' as a line–circle meet, show it satisfies both conditions, conclude X'=X.

name: Reflection Technique
one_liner: Reflect a point/line to straighten paths or reveal hidden congruence.
trigger: shortest-path, orthocenter reflections, or symmetric configuration
example_crux: Reflect the orthocenter over a side to land it on the circumcircle.

name: Rotation Technique
one_liner: Rotate part of a figure to transport lengths and create equal segments.
trigger: equilateral/square structure or equal segments at fixed angles
example_crux: Rotate 60° about a vertex to merge two segments into a straight line.

name: Stewart's Theorem
one_liner: Relates a cevian's length to the sides and the segments it cuts on the base.
trigger: length of a cevian given the base division
example_crux: Plug side and segment lengths into man+dad=bmb+cnc to get the cevian length.

name: Law of Cosines / Sines
one_liner: Connects side lengths with the cosine/sine of the opposite angle.
trigger: one angle and surrounding sides, or a side–angle conversion
example_crux: Solve for an unknown side using c²=a²+b²−2ab·cosC.

name: Pascal's Theorem
one_liner: Opposite sides of a hexagon inscribed in a conic meet in three collinear points.
trigger: six points on a circle/conic and a collinearity to prove
example_crux: Apply Pascal to a cleverly ordered hexagon to force three intersections collinear.

name: Brianchon's Theorem
one_liner: The main diagonals of a hexagon circumscribed about a conic concur.
trigger: six tangent lines to a conic and a concurrency to prove
example_crux: Dualize a tangential hexagon so its three diagonals meet at one point.

name: Desargues's Theorem
one_liner: Two triangles are perspective from a point iff perspective from a line.
trigger: two triangles with lines through corresponding vertices concurrent
example_crux: Convert a vertex-perspectivity into the collinearity of three side-intersections.

name: Monge's Theorem
one_liner: The three external homothety centers of three circles are collinear.
trigger: three circles and their pairwise external tangent intersections
example_crux: Show three external centers collinear by composing homotheties.

name: Casey's Theorem
one_liner: A Ptolemy-type relation among tangent lengths of four circles tangent to a fifth.
trigger: circles tangent to a common circle with tangent-length relation
example_crux: Replace side lengths by tangent lengths in a generalized Ptolemy relation.

name: Carnot's Theorem
one_liner: Perpendiculars to the sides at given feet concur iff an alternating sum of squares vanishes.
trigger: three perpendiculars to sides and a concurrency question
example_crux: Check the ±(x²−y²) sum equals zero to prove three perpendiculars concurrent.

name: Moving Points / Animation
one_liner: Animate one point projectively and verify a claim at enough special positions.
trigger: a claim linear/projective in one free point
example_crux: Show both sides trace projective maps agreeing at three positions, hence always.

name: Brocard Points & Angle
one_liner: Special interior points making equal angles with all three sides.
trigger: equal angles φ formed with successive sides
example_crux: Use cotφ = cotA+cotB+cotC to compute the Brocard angle.

name: Feuerbach's Theorem
one_liner: The nine-point circle is tangent to the incircle and the three excircles.
trigger: tangency between nine-point circle and incircle/excircles
example_crux: Compute the distance between centers equals the difference of radii to prove tangency.

name: Erdős–Mordell Inequality
one_liner: For an interior point, the sum of distances to vertices is at least twice the sum to the sides.
trigger: inequality comparing distances to vertices vs. to sides
example_crux: Bound each vertex distance below by weighted side distances and sum.

name: Trigonometric Substitution
one_liner: Replace constrained variables by triangle angles or sines to unlock identities.
trigger: constraint like a²+b²+c²+2abc=1 or an angle-sum condition
example_crux: Set a=cosA etc. so the constraint becomes A+B+C=π and apply Jensen.

name: Isoperimetric Inequality
one_liner: Among figures of fixed perimeter the circle (or regular polygon) maximizes area.
trigger: maximize area for fixed perimeter or vice versa
example_crux: Argue the optimal region must be convex and symmetric, forcing the circular case.

name: Equal Tangent Lengths
one_liner: Tangents from a point to a circle are equal, giving the incircle contact-length formulas.
trigger: incircle/excircle touch points and side segment lengths
example_crux: Set the two tangent segments from each vertex equal to get s−a contact lengths.

name: Tangent–Chord Angle
one_liner: The angle between a tangent and chord equals the inscribed angle in the alternate segment.
trigger: a tangent line meeting a chord at the point of tangency
example_crux: Transfer the tangent–chord angle to an inscribed angle to continue chasing.

name: SOS / Schur for Geometry
one_liner: Reduce a symmetric geometric inequality to a sum of squares in side lengths.
trigger: symmetric inequality in a,b,c after Ravi substitution
example_crux: Ravi-substitute a=y+z etc. and rewrite as ∑S_a(b−c)² ≥ 0.

name: Ravi Substitution
one_liner: Replace triangle sides a,b,c with x+y, y+z, z+x to remove triangle constraints.
trigger: inequality valid only for triangle side lengths
example_crux: Substitute positive x,y,z so the triangle inequality is automatic, then apply AM-GM.

name: Length/Angle Chasing with Ratios
one_liner: Chain similar triangles and ratio equalities to compute an unknown length.
trigger: a network of similar triangles or parallel lines
example_crux: Cascade several pairs of similar triangles to express one segment via known ones.

name: Lifting the Exponent
one_liner: Computes the exact prime-power dividing a^n ± b^n via valuations of a±b and n.
trigger: need v_p of a^n ± b^n for large n
example_crux: Determine the largest power of a prime dividing a difference of nth powers by adding v_p(a-b) and v_p(n).

name: Vieta Jumping
one_liner: Uses the two roots of a quadratic in one variable to descend to a smaller solution.
trigger: symmetric Diophantine relation with a quadratic in each variable
example_crux: Assume a minimal solution and produce a smaller one via the conjugate root to force a contradiction or fixed value.

name: Chicken McNugget (Frobenius)
one_liner: Gives the largest integer not representable as a nonnegative combination of coprime a and b.
trigger: which values are (not) representable as ax+by with x,y≥0
example_crux: The largest non-representable amount with coprime coin sizes is ab-a-b.

name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
one_liner: Reconstructs an integer modulo a product from its residues modulo coprime factors.
trigger: constraints given by several coprime moduli simultaneously
example_crux: Build a number hitting prescribed residues mod each prime to guarantee existence of a desired configuration.

name: Fermat's Little Theorem
one_liner: States a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p) for gcd(a,p)=1.
trigger: exponent reduction modulo a prime
example_crux: Reduce a huge exponent mod p-1 to evaluate a power mod p.

name: Euler's Theorem / Totient
one_liner: Generalizes Fermat: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) when gcd(a,n)=1.
trigger: exponent reduction modulo a composite
example_crux: Compute a tower of exponents mod n by iterating totients.

name: Multiplicative Order
one_liner: Uses the order of a mod n dividing any exponent giving 1 to constrain periods.
trigger: when does a^k ≡ 1 (mod n)
example_crux: Show the order divides both k and φ(n) to pin down the exponent structure.

name: Primitive Roots
one_liner: Represents the multiplicative group mod p as cyclic generated by one element.
trigger: need a generator or discrete-log structure mod p
example_crux: Convert a multiplicative condition into an additive one on exponents via a primitive root.

name: Wilson's Theorem
one_liner: States (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p) iff p is prime.
trigger: factorials mod a prime or primality test
example_crux: Pair inverses among 1..p-1 leaving only the self-inverse factors to evaluate a factorial.

name: Quadratic Residues
one_liner: Classifies which residues are squares mod p to rule out solvability.
trigger: an equation forces a square in a modulus
example_crux: Show no solution because a required value is a nonresidue mod a chosen prime.

name: Quadratic Reciprocity
one_liner: Relates solvability of x²≡p (mod q) and x²≡q (mod p) via Legendre symbols.
trigger: compute a Legendre/Jacobi symbol quickly
example_crux: Flip Legendre symbols to reduce to a small residue computation.

name: Hensel's Lemma
one_liner: Lifts a simple root mod p to a root mod p^k.
trigger: solution exists mod p and want it mod higher power
example_crux: Nonsingular root mod p uniquely lifts to every power of p.

name: p-adic Valuation Bookkeeping
one_liner: Tracks exponents of a prime through products, sums, and factorials.
trigger: divisibility questions with explicit prime powers
example_crux: Compare v_p of both sides of an equation to force equality or contradiction.

name: Legendre's Formula
one_liner: Gives v_p(n!) = sum of floor(n/p^k).
trigger: prime power dividing a factorial or binomial
example_crux: Use (n - s_p(n))/(p-1) to count prime factors of a factorial.

name: Kummer's Theorem
one_liner: v_p of a binomial equals the number of carries when adding in base p.
trigger: prime power dividing C(m+n, n)
example_crux: Count base-p carries to get the exact power dividing a binomial coefficient.

name: Lucas' Theorem
one_liner: Computes C(m,n) mod p from base-p digit binomials.
trigger: binomial coefficient modulo a prime
example_crux: A binomial is nonzero mod p iff each base-p digit of n is at most that of m.

name: Infinite Descent
one_liner: Derives a contradiction by producing an endless chain of ever-smaller solutions.
trigger: prove no nontrivial integer solutions exist
example_crux: From any positive solution build a strictly smaller one, impossible in the naturals.

name: Sum of Two Squares (Descent)
one_liner: Characterizes integers expressible as x²+y² via primes ≡ 1 (mod 4).
trigger: represent n as a sum of two squares
example_crux: A prime ≡1 mod 4 is a sum of two squares by Gaussian-integer factorization or descent.

name: Gaussian Integers
one_liner: Factors quadratic forms in Z[i] where unique factorization applies.
trigger: expression factors as x²+y² or norm form
example_crux: Factor x²+y²=(x+iy)(x-iy) and use unique factorization in Z[i].

name: Pell's Equation
one_liner: Generates all solutions of x²-Dy²=1 from a fundamental unit.
trigger: quadratic Diophantine of Pell type
example_crux: Powers of the fundamental solution yield infinitely many, obeying a linear recurrence.

name: Continued Fractions
one_liner: Expands a real number to get best rational approximations and Pell solutions.
trigger: best approximations or solving Pell
example_crux: Convergents give the fundamental Pell solution and near-equality bounds.

name: Euclidean Algorithm / Bézout
one_liner: Computes gcd and expresses it as an integer combination ax+by.
trigger: need gcd structure or a linear representation
example_crux: Reduce gcd(a,b) via remainders and back-substitute to write gcd = ax+by.

name: gcd of Sequence Terms
one_liner: Exploits gcd(a_m,a_n)=a_{gcd(m,n)} type identities for structured sequences.
trigger: gcd of Fibonacci/Mersenne-like terms
example_crux: gcd(2^m-1,2^n-1)=2^{gcd(m,n)}-1 reduces the problem to index gcd.

name: Modular Contradiction
one_liner: Rules out solutions by evaluating an equation modulo a well-chosen number.
trigger: show a Diophantine equation has no solutions
example_crux: Reduce mod 4, 8, or 9 to find both sides can't match.

name: Zsygmondy's Theorem
one_liner: Guarantees a new prime factor of a^n-b^n except small exceptions.
trigger: need a prime dividing a^n-b^n but no smaller term
example_crux: A primitive prime divisor exists, forcing infinitely many distinct primes.

name: Cyclotomic Polynomials
one_liner: Factors x^n-1 into Φ_d and links prime divisors to orders.
trigger: prime divisors of a^n-1 or order conditions
example_crux: A prime dividing Φ_n(a) is ≡1 mod n or divides n, controlling divisors.

name: Dirichlet's Theorem on Primes in AP
one_liner: There are infinitely many primes in any coprime arithmetic progression.
trigger: need a prime of a specific residue class
example_crux: Invoke infinitely many primes ≡ a (mod m) to select one with needed properties.

name: Pigeonhole on Residues
one_liner: Forces two elements sharing a residue to obtain a divisibility.
trigger: many integers, few residue classes
example_crux: Among n+1 numbers two share a residue mod n, so their difference is divisible.

name: Thue's Lemma
one_liner: Finds small x,y with ax≡y using pigeonhole on a lattice box.
trigger: represent residues by small numerator/denominator
example_crux: Pigeonhole yields |x|,|y|<√p with x≡ay, key to sum-of-two-squares.

name: Telescoping / Partial Fractions
one_liner: Collapses a sum via cancellation of consecutive terms.
trigger: sum of a difference-shaped term
example_crux: Write each term as f(k+1)-f(k) so the sum collapses to endpoints.

name: Generating Functions
one_liner: Encodes counting sequences as coefficients of a formal power series.
trigger: count representations or partitions
example_crux: Read off a representation count as a coefficient in a product of series.

name: Roots of Unity Filter
one_liner: Extracts terms in a fixed residue class of exponents via nth roots of unity.
trigger: sum of coefficients with index ≡ r (mod m)
example_crux: Average f(ω^j) over roots of unity to isolate every mth coefficient.

name: Multiplicative Functions
one_liner: Evaluates functions like φ, σ, μ via their values on prime powers.
trigger: sum or product over divisors of a multiplicative function
example_crux: Factor n and multiply local values to get the arithmetic function globally.

name: Möbius Inversion
one_liner: Inverts a divisor-sum relation to recover the original function.
trigger: F(n)=Σ_{d|n} f(d) and you want f
example_crux: f(n)=Σ_{d|n} μ(n/d)F(d) untangles a divisor-sum identity.

name: Dirichlet Convolution
one_liner: Combines arithmetic functions via (f*g)(n)=Σ_{d|n} f(d)g(n/d).
trigger: manipulate identities among arithmetic functions
example_crux: Recognize a divisor sum as a convolution and use its ring structure.

name: Size/Growth Bounding
one_liner: Traps a solution between consecutive values (e.g., squares) to force it.
trigger: a value must be a perfect power in a narrow range
example_crux: Show n² < N < (n+1)² so N cannot be a perfect square.

name: Algebraic Factoring (SFFT)
one_liner: Rewrites an equation as a product to enumerate divisor cases.
trigger: equation reshapes into (x+a)(y+b)=c
example_crux: Simon's Favorite Factoring turns xy+ax+by into a factored product with finite divisor cases.

name: Sum-of-Squares Representation
one_liner: Expresses a quantity as a sum of squares to force nonnegativity or specific values.
trigger: need a nonnegativity or vanishing argument
example_crux: A vanishing sum of squares forces each square to be zero.

name: Parity / Mod 2 Argument
one_liner: Uses even/odd structure to rule out or force configurations.
trigger: counting or equation with hidden even/odd invariance
example_crux: Odd cannot equal even, killing a candidate equation.

name: Base-b Representation
one_liner: Analyzes numbers through their digits in a chosen base.
trigger: digit sums, carries, or base conversions
example_crux: Use base-p digits to compute carries or digit sums controlling divisibility.

name: Digit-Sum Invariance (Casting Out Nines)
one_liner: Uses n ≡ digit sum (mod 9) to constrain digit configurations.
trigger: digit-sum or mod 9/11 conditions
example_crux: Match a number and its digit sum mod 9 to exclude impossible digits.

name: Extremal Principle / Well-Ordering
one_liner: Takes a minimal counterexample or extreme solution to derive structure.
trigger: existence proof via a smallest object
example_crux: Consider the least solution and contradict minimality.

name: Strong / Structural Induction
one_liner: Builds results for all n from smaller cases, sometimes on prime factorization.
trigger: statement parametrized by n or divisor structure
example_crux: Reduce n to its factors or n-1 and apply the inductive hypothesis.

name: Rational Root / Integer Root Analysis
one_liner: Constrains integer roots to divisors of the constant term.
trigger: integer solutions of a polynomial equation
example_crux: Any integer root divides the constant term, limiting candidates.

name: a-b divides P(a)-P(b)
one_liner: Uses that integer polynomials respect differences: (a-b)|(P(a)-P(b)).
trigger: divisibility among polynomial values
example_crux: Deduce constraints on P by forcing differences of arguments to divide differences of values.

name: Eisenstein's Criterion
one_liner: Proves irreducibility over Q via a prime dividing all but the leading coefficient.
trigger: show a polynomial is irreducible
example_crux: A prime p with p∤a_n, p|a_i, p²∤a_0 forces irreducibility.

name: Reduction mod p for Polynomials
one_liner: Studies a polynomial's factorization or roots in F_p to gain global info.
trigger: count roots or test factorization via a prime
example_crux: A degree-d polynomial has at most d roots mod p, bounding solution counts.

name: Double Counting Divisor Sums
one_liner: Counts pairs (n,d) with d|n two ways to derive identities.
trigger: sum over n of divisor-related quantity
example_crux: Σ_{n≤N} d(n) = Σ_d floor(N/d) by counting lattice points under a hyperbola.

name: Smoothing / Adjustment
one_liner: Perturbs a candidate extremal configuration toward optimum without loss.
trigger: optimize an arithmetic quantity over configurations
example_crux: Adjust two values keeping constraints while improving the objective.

name: Probabilistic / Counting Existence
one_liner: Shows an object exists because a random or average choice works.
trigger: existence among many number-theoretic configurations
example_crux: Expected count of good residues is positive, so one exists.

name: Density / Counting Bound
one_liner: Compares set sizes or densities to force overlaps or impossibility.
trigger: a set is too large/small to avoid a property
example_crux: A set exceeding half the residues must contain two summing to a target.

name: Unique Factorization
one_liner: Uses uniqueness of prime factorization to compare exponents across an equation.
trigger: equation of products of integers
example_crux: Match prime exponents on both sides to force structure.

name: Coprime Factor Argument
one_liner: When coprime factors multiply to a power, each factor is itself a power.
trigger: product of coprimes equals a perfect power
example_crux: If ab=c^k with gcd(a,b)=1, both a and b are kth powers.

name: Jacobi Symbol Computation
one_liner: Extends Legendre symbols multiplicatively to compute residue characters fast.
trigger: evaluate a quadratic character with composite modulus
example_crux: Apply reciprocity to a Jacobi symbol to decide solvability quickly.

name: Chevalley–Warning Theorem
one_liner: Guarantees the number of solutions mod p is divisible by p under degree bounds.
trigger: solution count of polynomial system over F_p
example_crux: Low total degree forces a nontrivial common zero mod p.

name: Stars and Bars / Composition Counting
one_liner: Counts nonnegative integer solutions of linear equations.
trigger: count solutions of x1+...+xk=n
example_crux: Number of nonnegative solutions is C(n+k-1,k-1).

name: Gauss Sums / Character Sums
one_liner: Evaluates weighted exponential sums over residues using characters.
trigger: sums of Legendre symbols or additive characters
example_crux: The quadratic Gauss sum has magnitude √p, controlling residue distributions.

name: Euclid-style Prime Construction
one_liner: Constructs a number forcing a new prime factor to prove infinitude.
trigger: prove infinitely many primes of some form
example_crux: N+1 or a cyclotomic value must have a prime factor outside a finite list.

name: Order Argument for Prime Divisors
one_liner: Uses ord_p to show prime divisors of a^n±1 lie in fixed residue classes.
trigger: classify primes dividing a^n±1
example_crux: A prime dividing a^n+1 has order 2n, so p≡1 mod 2n.

name: Vieta Jumping
one_liner: Uses the two roots of a quadratic in one variable to descend to a smaller solution or bound extremal ones.
trigger: symmetric integer equation with a constant target and hidden quadratic structure
example_crux: Show (a^2+b^2)/(ab+1) is a perfect square by minimizing a+b and flipping a root.

name: Simon's Favorite Factoring Trick
one_liner: Adds a constant to complete a product like xy+ax+by=(x+b)(y+a)-ab.
trigger: equation with a product term plus linear terms in two variables
example_crux: Solve xy=x+y+3 by writing (x-1)(y-1)=4 and listing divisors.

name: AM-GM Inequality
one_liner: The arithmetic mean of nonnegatives is at least their geometric mean.
trigger: product to bound by a sum or vice versa
example_crux: Minimize x+1/x for x>0 via x+1/x>=2.

name: Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
one_liner: Bounds a dot product by the product of norms: (Σa_ib_i)^2 ≤ (Σa_i^2)(Σb_i^2).
trigger: sum of products to split into two square sums
example_crux: Bound (a+b+c)^2 ≤ 3(a^2+b^2+c^2) as a Cauchy-Schwarz instance.

name: Titu's Lemma (Engel Form)
one_liner: Σ a_i^2/b_i ≥ (Σa_i)^2 / Σb_i for positive b_i.
trigger: sum of fractions with squared numerators
example_crux: Prove a/(b+c)+b/(c+a)+c/(a+b) ≥ 3/2 via squared-numerator form.

name: Power Mean Inequality
one_liner: Higher-order means dominate lower-order means of the same data.
trigger: comparing quadratic, arithmetic, geometric, harmonic averages
example_crux: Deduce QM ≥ AM ≥ GM ≥ HM for fixed positive reals.

name: Rearrangement Inequality
one_liner: A sum of products is maximized when both sequences are sorted the same way.
trigger: sum of pairwise products of two ordered sequences
example_crux: Show Σ a_i b_{σ(i)} ≤ Σ a_i b_i for similarly sorted sequences.

name: Chebyshev's Sum Inequality
one_liner: For similarly sorted sequences, n·Σa_ib_i ≥ (Σa_i)(Σb_i).
trigger: average of products vs product of averages for monotone data
example_crux: Bound Σ a_i^2 relative to (Σa_i)^2/n using monotone alignment.

name: Jensen's Inequality
one_liner: For convex f, f(mean) ≤ mean(f); reversed for concave.
trigger: sum of a convex/concave function applied to variables with fixed sum
example_crux: Prove Σ 1/(1+x_i) inequalities by convexity when Σx_i is fixed.

name: Muirhead's Inequality
one_liner: Symmetric-sum inequality governed by majorization of exponent tuples.
trigger: symmetric sums of monomials with comparable exponent vectors
example_crux: Compare Σ_sym a^2b to Σ_sym abc via (2,1,0) majorizing (1,1,1).

name: Karamata's Inequality
one_liner: If x majorizes y and f is convex, then Σf(x_i) ≥ Σf(y_i).
trigger: convex function summed over majorization-ordered tuples
example_crux: Prove a symmetric convex-sum inequality by verifying the majorization order.

name: Schur's Inequality
one_liner: For nonnegative a,b,c and t>0, Σ a^t(a-b)(a-c) ≥ 0.
trigger: three-variable symmetric inequality resistant to AM-GM
example_crux: Close the gap in a^3+b^3+c^3+abc bounds using the t=1 Schur term.

name: Hölder's Inequality
one_liner: Generalizes Cauchy-Schwarz across several sequences with conjugate exponents.
trigger: need to combine three or more sums or cube-type structure
example_crux: Prove (Σa)(Σb)(Σc) ≥ (Σ(abc)^{1/3})^3 to attack a cyclic fraction sum.

name: Minkowski's Inequality
one_liner: Triangle inequality for L^p norms of vectors.
trigger: sum of square roots of sums of squares
example_crux: Bound Σ sqrt(a_i^2+b_i^2) by sqrt((Σa_i)^2+(Σb_i)^2).

name: Tangent Line Trick
one_liner: Bounds a convex/concave term by its tangent line at the equality point.
trigger: single-variable-summed inequality with known equality case
example_crux: Replace f(x_i) by its tangent a·x_i+b at 1 to linearize a symmetric sum.

name: Sum of Squares (SOS) Method
one_liner: Rewrites a symmetric inequality as Σ S_a(b-c)^2 ≥ 0 and analyzes coefficients.
trigger: symmetric three-variable inequality with equality at a=b=c
example_crux: Express a difference as S_a(b-c)^2+S_b(c-a)^2+S_c(a-b)^2 and check positivity.

name: uvw / pqr Method
one_liner: Reparametrizes symmetric expressions in elementary symmetric quantities to reduce to boundary cases.
trigger: symmetric inequality where extremes occur at equal or zero variables
example_crux: Fix p=a+b+c and q=ab+bc+ca, then optimize over r=abc at its boundary.

name: Ravi Substitution
one_liner: Substitutes triangle sides a=y+z, b=z+x, c=x+y to remove triangle constraints.
trigger: inequality among triangle side lengths
example_crux: Turn a side-length inequality into a positive-variable AM-GM problem.

name: Normalization & Homogenization
one_liner: Rescales variables or equalizes degrees to remove a constraint like a+b+c=1.
trigger: constraint tying variables together in an inequality
example_crux: Set a+b+c=1 (or homogenize) so both sides share the same degree.

name: Smoothing / Mixing Variables
one_liner: Moves two variables toward each other without decreasing the objective until the extremum.
trigger: symmetric optimum expected at equal or boundary values
example_crux: Show replacing (a,b) by their average improves the expression, forcing a=b=c.

name: Lagrange Multipliers
one_liner: Finds constrained extrema where the gradient is a combination of constraint gradients.
trigger: smooth constrained optimization needing candidate critical points
example_crux: Locate the extremum of a product subject to a sum constraint via ∇f=λ∇g.

name: Weighted AM-GM
one_liner: Weighted arithmetic mean dominates weighted geometric mean.
trigger: need unequal exponents in a product-vs-sum bound
example_crux: Split a^3 as weighted average to match target monomial in a mixed sum.

name: Abel Summation
one_liner: Discrete integration by parts rewriting Σa_kb_k using partial sums of one factor.
trigger: sum of products where one factor is monotone or telescopes
example_crux: Bound Σ a_k b_k by transferring differences onto partial sums of a_k.

name: Telescoping
one_liner: Writes terms as consecutive differences so a sum collapses to endpoints.
trigger: sum or product with a suspected difference/ratio form

name: Partial Fraction Decomposition
one_liner: Splits a rational function into simpler fractions to enable telescoping or summation.
trigger: rational term with factorable denominator inside a sum
example_crux: Decompose 1/(k^2−1) into halves to telescope the series.

name: Roots of Unity Filter
one_liner: Extracts coefficients in an arithmetic progression by averaging over roots of unity.
trigger: count terms with index ≡ r (mod m) in an expansion
example_crux: Sum binomials C(n,k) with k≡0 mod 3 via (2^n+2·(-1)-...)/3 using ω.

name: Generating Functions
one_liner: Encodes a sequence as coefficients of a formal power series to manipulate it algebraically.
trigger: recurrence or counting identity resisting direct summation
example_crux: Solve a Fibonacci-type recurrence via x/(1−x−x^2).

name: Lagrange Interpolation
one_liner: Reconstructs a degree-n polynomial from n+1 point values.
trigger: polynomial known only at specific inputs but a value is needed elsewhere
example_crux: Given P(k)=1/k for k=1..n, find P(n+1) by interpolating.

name: Finite Differences
one_liner: Uses the difference operator to detect polynomial degree and closed forms.
trigger: sequence suspected polynomial or needing summation of powers
example_crux: Show (n+1)-st differences of a degree-n polynomial sequence vanish.

name: Newton's Identities
one_liner: Relates power sums to elementary symmetric polynomials recursively.
trigger: need power sums of roots from coefficients or vice versa
example_crux: Compute a^3+b^3+c^3 from e_1,e_2,e_3 of the roots.

name: Vieta's Formulas
one_liner: Expresses symmetric functions of roots via polynomial coefficients.
trigger: roots' sums/products needed without solving the polynomial
example_crux: Use r+s=-b/a, rs=c/a to evaluate an expression in the roots.

name: Symmetric Polynomial Reduction
one_liner: Rewrites any symmetric polynomial in elementary symmetric polynomials.
trigger: symmetric expression to express through e_1,e_2,...
example_crux: Convert Σ a^2b^2 into a formula in e_1,e_2,e_3.

name: Cauchy Functional Equation
one_liner: f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y) forces linearity under regularity or positivity constraints.
trigger: additive-looking functional equation over reals or rationals
example_crux: Deduce f(x)=cx from additivity plus monotonicity or boundedness.

name: Strategic Substitution (FE)
one_liner: Plugs special values (0, 1, x=y, swaps) to extract structural information.
trigger: general functional equation needing initial data
example_crux: Set x=y=0 to find f(0), then substitute to get injectivity.

name: Characteristic Equation
one_liner: Solves linear recurrences via roots of an associated polynomial.
trigger: linear recurrence with constant coefficients
example_crux: Solve a_n=3a_{n-1}-2a_{n-2} using roots 1 and 2.

name: Rational Root Theorem
one_liner: Rational roots p/q must have p | constant term and q | leading coefficient.
trigger: integer polynomial whose rational roots are sought or excluded
example_crux: Rule out rational roots to prove an integer polynomial is irreducible over Q.

name: Gauss's Lemma / Integer Factoring
one_liner: A primitive polynomial factoring over Q factors over Z.
trigger: irreducibility or integer factorization of a polynomial
example_crux: Reduce factoring over Q to factoring over Z to apply mod-p arguments.

name: Discriminant Method
one_liner: Uses the sign/value of a quadratic's discriminant to constrain solutions.
trigger: existence of real roots or an integer being a perfect square
example_crux: Force b^2−4ac to be a perfect square for integer roots.

name: Infinitely Many Roots Argument
one_liner: A polynomial vanishing at infinitely many points is identically zero.
trigger: polynomial identity holding for all n or infinitely many values
example_crux: Prove two polynomials equal by matching them on all integers.

name: Comparing Coefficients
one_liner: Matches coefficients of equal polynomials/series to get a system of equations.
trigger: identity between two polynomial or power-series expressions
example_crux: Equate x^k coefficients on both sides of a product identity.

name: Chebyshev Polynomials
one_liner: T_n(cosθ)=cos(nθ) gives extremal minimal-deviation polynomials.
trigger: cosine multiple-angle identities or minimax polynomial bounds
example_crux: Use T_n's leading behavior to bound a monic polynomial's sup norm on [-1,1].

name: Cyclotomic Polynomials
one_liner: Factors x^n−1 into irreducible pieces Φ_d over the integers.
trigger: problems about x^n−1, primitive roots of unity, or orders
example_crux: Use Φ_p(x) irreducibility to analyze divisibility of n^k−1.

name: Complex Numbers on the Unit Circle
one_liner: Encodes angles and rotations via e^{iθ} to simplify trig sums and geometry.
trigger: sum of cosines/sines or rotational symmetry
example_crux: Evaluate Σ cos(kθ) as the real part of a geometric series in e^{iθ}.

name: Substitution t = x + 1/x
one_liner: Reduces a symmetric (palindromic) equation's degree by a reciprocal substitution.
trigger: palindromic polynomial or expression symmetric in x and 1/x
example_crux: Turn x^4+ax^3+bx^2+ax+1=0 into a quadratic in t=x+1/x.

name: Telescoping Products
one_liner: Writes factors as ratios so a product collapses to boundary terms.
trigger: product with a suspected consecutive-ratio structure
example_crux: Evaluate Π (1−1/k^2) via factoring into (k-1)(k+1)/k^2 ratios.

name: Descartes' Rule of Signs
one_liner: Bounds positive real roots by the number of sign changes in coefficients.
trigger: counting or excluding real roots of a polynomial
example_crux: Show a polynomial has no positive roots since all coefficients are positive.

name: Bounding / Squeeze
one_liner: Traps a quantity between consecutive integers or matching bounds to pin it down.
trigger: expression forced to be an integer or between tight limits
example_crux: Show n^2 < N < (n+1)^2 so N is not a perfect square.

name: Extremal Principle
one_liner: Considers a maximal/minimal object to force a contradiction or structure.
trigger: existence claim or minimal counterexample setup
example_crux: Take the smallest solution and construct a smaller one for contradiction.

name: Invariant / Monovariant
one_liner: Tracks a quantity preserved (or monotone) under allowed operations.
trigger: process with moves where final vs initial state is questioned
example_crux: Show a sum mod 2 stays fixed, so a target configuration is unreachable.

name: Strong Induction
one_liner: Proves a statement using all smaller cases, not just the immediate predecessor.
trigger: recursive structure needing multiple prior cases
example_crux: Prove a recurrence bound assuming it for every smaller index.

name: Bernoulli's Inequality
one_liner: (1+x)^n ≥ 1+nx for x ≥ −1 and n ≥ 1.
trigger: power of a near-one quantity needing a linear lower bound
example_crux: Lower-bound (1+1/n)^n to compare growth rates.

name: SOS-Schur Technique
one_liner: Combines SOS coefficient analysis with Schur-type terms to certify positivity.
trigger: SOS coefficients not all nonnegative but WLOG ordering helps
example_crux: Assume a≥b≥c and bound negative S_b term by neighboring positive ones.

name: Trigonometric Substitution
one_liner: Replaces constrained variables by trig functions to exploit identities.
trigger: constraint like a^2+b^2=1 or a+b+c=abc pattern
example_crux: Set a=tanA etc. when a+b+c=abc to invoke triangle angle identities.

name: Matrix / Linear Algebra Encoding
one_liner: Encodes recurrences or transformations as matrix powers to find closed forms.
trigger: vector-valued recurrence or repeated linear map
example_crux: Express Fibonacci pairs as powers of [[1,1],[1,0]] to get identities.

name: De Moivre's Theorem
one_liner: Raises complex numbers in polar form to powers, converting angle multiplication into exponent addition.
trigger: powers of cos/sin or expanding cos(nθ) in terms of cosθ
example_crux: Expand (cosθ+isinθ)^n and compare real/imaginary parts to derive cos(nθ) and sin(nθ) formulas.

name: Euler's Formula
one_liner: Encodes trigonometric quantities as e^{iθ}=cosθ+isinθ so trig identities become algebra of exponentials.
trigger: products/sums of many sines and cosines
example_crux: Write cosθ=(e^{iθ}+e^{-iθ})/2 to turn a trig product into a geometric sum.

name: Roots of Unity
one_liner: Uses the n-th roots of unity and their symmetric algebraic relations to evaluate sums and products.
trigger: symmetric sums over equally spaced angles or nth roots
example_crux: Evaluate a product of sines by pairing them with factors of z^n-1 at roots of unity.

name: Roots of Unity Filter
one_liner: Extracts coefficients in arithmetic-progression residue classes by averaging a generating function over roots of unity.
trigger: sum of binomial coefficients every k-th term
example_crux: Compute the sum of C(n,k) with k≡0 mod 3 by averaging (1+ω^j)^n over cube roots of unity.

name: Tangent Half-Angle Substitution
one_liner: Sets t=tan(θ/2) to rewrite all trig functions as rational functions of t.
trigger: trig equation or integral to rationalize
example_crux: Solve a·cosθ+b·sinθ=c by substituting t=tan(θ/2) to get a quadratic in t.

name: Product-to-Sum Formulas
one_liner: Converts products of sines/cosines into sums, enabling telescoping or cancellation.
trigger: a product of two trig factors blocking a sum
example_crux: Rewrite 2sinA·cosB as sin(A+B)+sin(A−B) to collapse a summation.

name: Sum-to-Product (Prosthaphaeresis)
one_liner: Turns a sum/difference of trig values into a product, exposing common factors.
trigger: factoring sinA±sinB or cosA±cosB
example_crux: Factor cosA−cosB=−2sin((A+B)/2)sin((A−B)/2) to solve an equation.

name: Auxiliary Angle Method
one_liner: Rewrites a·cosθ+b·sinθ as R·cos(θ−φ) to find extrema and solve linear trig equations.
trigger: linear combination of sine and cosine
example_crux: Find the max of 3cosθ+4sinθ by writing it as 5cos(θ−φ).

name: Chebyshev Polynomials
one_liner: Expresses cos(nθ) as a degree-n polynomial in cosθ satisfying a linear recurrence.
trigger: cos(nθ) as polynomial or minimal-deviation extremal problems
example_crux: Use T_n(cosθ)=cos(nθ) and the recurrence T_{n+1}=2xT_n−T_{n-1} to find roots as cosines.

name: Cotangent/Tangent Telescoping
one_liner: Writes each summand as a difference cot(x)−cot(2x)-type so a trig series collapses.
trigger: sum of 1/(sin·sin) or arctan-like trig terms
example_crux: Use 1/(sin kθ·sin(k+1)θ)=(cot kθ−cot(k+1)θ)/sinθ to telescope the sum.

name: Lagrange Trigonometric Identity
one_liner: Gives closed forms for Σcos(kθ) and Σsin(kθ) via the Dirichlet-kernel geometric sum.
trigger: sum of cosines/sines in arithmetic progression
example_crux: Sum cosθ+cos2θ+…+cos nθ by summing the geometric series of e^{ikθ}.

name: Multiple-Angle Formulas
one_liner: Expands cos(nθ), sin(nθ), tan(nθ) in terms of single-angle functions.
trigger: relating a multiple angle to a base angle
example_crux: Solve cos3θ=4cos³θ−3cosθ set equal to a target value.

name: Law of Sines
one_liner: Relates each side of a triangle to the sine of its opposite angle and the circumradius.
trigger: mixing sides and opposite angles
example_crux: Convert side ratios to sine ratios via a/sinA=2R to resolve an angle chase.

name: Law of Cosines
one_liner: Relates one side to the other two and the included angle, generalizing Pythagoras.
trigger: one angle with all three sides
example_crux: Compute a missing side or angle from c²=a²+b²−2ab·cosC.

name: Ptolemy's Theorem
one_liner: For a cyclic quadrilateral, the product of diagonals equals the sum of products of opposite sides.
trigger: cyclic quadrilateral with mixed side/diagonal products
example_crux: Apply AC·BD=AB·CD+AD·BC in a cyclic quadrilateral to prove a length relation.

name: Trigonometric Ceva
one_liner: Gives concurrency of cevians via a product of ratios of sines of split angles equaling one.
trigger: concurrency of cevians described by angles
example_crux: Prove three cevians concur by verifying the product of sin-ratios of the six angles equals 1.

name: Arctangent Addition/Telescoping
one_liner: Combines arctan terms via the tangent addition formula so a sum telescopes to a single angle.
trigger: sum of arctan(1/(something quadratic))
example_crux: Write arctan(1/(k²+k+1))=arctan(k+1)−arctan(k) to collapse the series.

name: Tangent Line Trick
one_liner: Bounds a convex/concave function by its tangent line at the equality point to prove a sum inequality.
trigger: symmetric inequality tight at equal variables
example_crux: Bound f(x)≥ax+b tangent at x=1/3 to sum a constrained cyclic inequality.

name: Jensen's Inequality (Trig)
one_liner: Uses convexity/concavity to bound sums like Σsin(A) over a triangle's angles.
trigger: symmetric trig sum with fixed angle sum
example_crux: Since sin is concave on (0,π), sinA+sinB+sinC≤3sin(π/3) for triangle angles.

name: Sum of Squares (SOS)
one_liner: Rewrites a difference as a sum of squared terms times nonnegative coefficients to prove nonnegativity.
trigger: symmetric inequality to certify as nonnegative
example_crux: Express LHS−RHS as Σ S_a(b−c)² with S_a≥0 to finish an inequality.

name: Vieta's Formulas
one_liner: Relates a polynomial's coefficients to symmetric functions of its roots.
trigger: symmetric expressions in roots or trig values as polynomial roots
example_crux: Recognize cos(2π/7), cos(4π/7), cos(6π/7) as roots of a cubic and read off their sum.

name: Newton's Identities
one_liner: Recursively links power sums of roots to elementary symmetric polynomials.
trigger: needing p_k=Σx_i^k from symmetric data
example_crux: Compute Σroot³ from e_1,e_2,e_3 via p_3=e_1p_2−e_2p_1+3e_3.

name: Complex Numbers in Geometry
one_liner: Places points as complex numbers so collinearity, angles, and similarity become algebra.
trigger: plane geometry with symmetric or rotational structure
example_crux: Prove a configuration is equilateral by checking a+ωb+ω²c=0 with ω a cube root of unity.

name: Rotation via Complex Multiplication
one_liner: Rotates a point about another by multiplying the difference by e^{iθ}.
trigger: spiral similarity or fixed-angle rotation of points
example_crux: Encode a 60° rotation as z↦c+(z−c)e^{iπ/3} to locate a constructed vertex.

name: Trigonometric Substitution
one_liner: Replaces an algebraic variable by sinθ, tanθ, etc., exploiting trig identities to simplify.
trigger: expressions like √(1−x²) or 1+x²
example_crux: Set x=tanθ so that 1+x² becomes sec²θ and a constraint linearizes.

name: Discriminant Method
one_liner: Uses the sign/vanishing of a quadratic's discriminant to bound a value or force equality.
trigger: an expression rearrangeable into a quadratic in one variable
example_crux: Treat the target as a quadratic in x with real roots, so its discriminant ≥0 gives the bound.

name: Partial Fractions
one_liner: Decomposes a rational term into simpler fractions that often telescope.
trigger: sum of reciprocals of factored products
example_crux: Split 1/(k(k+1)) as 1/k−1/(k+1) to telescope the sum.

name: Telescoping Products (Morrie-type)
one_liner: Multiplies a cosine product by a strategic sine to repeatedly halve angles and collapse it.
trigger: product of cosines at doubling angles
example_crux: Prove cos20°cos40°cos80°=1/8 by multiplying by sin20° and iterating the double-angle formula.

name: Symmetric Function Reduction
one_liner: Expresses symmetric expressions in terms of elementary symmetric polynomials to simplify.
trigger: cyclic/symmetric expression in several variables
example_crux: Rewrite a²+b²+c² as (a+b+c)²−2(ab+bc+ca) using symmetric basis.

name: Conjugate Multiplication
one_liner: Multiplies by a conjugate to rationalize or expose a telescoping difference.
trigger: differences of square roots or nested radicals
example_crux: Simplify 1/(√(k+1)+√k) to √(k+1)−√k to telescope.

name: Complex Geometric Series
one_liner: Sums a series of e^{ikθ} as a geometric progression to extract trig sums via real/imaginary parts.
trigger: finite sum of sines or cosines
